---
title: "Winter Olympics Medal Analysis"
author: "Octavia Steiger"
date: today

format:
    html:
        toc: true
        theme: cosmo
        code-fold: true

execute:
    warning: false
    message: false

jupyter: python3
---

## Research Question
What actually drives medal succes in the Winter Olympics? Is it just the wealth of a country, ie richer countries have more resources to invest in training and facilities or population size, ie more people means more potential athletes? Or does hosting the Games give a country an advantage?

To answer this, I needed to look at the medal counts over time, the economic data of each country competing and the hosting history of the Winter Olympics. In the sections below I walk through the data pipeline I used to answer this question and the steps I took to get to the final result. 

The short answer: yes to all three, but not equally. Wealth and population size are the strongest predictors of medal success, while hosting provides a measurable but smaller boost above and beyond those factors.

The data pipeline: 
scrape --> clean --> merge with World Bank data --> analyse --> visualise 

---

## 1. Collecting the Medal Data
The first step was to collect the medal data for each of the Winter Olympic Games all the way back to 1924. Offical Olympic datawas not available as a download, so the data had to be scraped directly from Olmpedia (https://www.olympedia.org). 

I built a web scraper (`scrape.py`) that fetched the medal table for each of the 24 Winter Olympic Games, making sure that I paused between requests to avoid overwhelming the server. Each page returned an HTML table listing every event alongside gold, silver and bronze medalists and their countries. 

The raw data came back in a wide format, with seperate columns for gold, silver and bronze medalists. At this point the data was still quite messy, with country names in different formats, sports categories mixed in as heading rows and some NOC fields had combined codes for when medals were tied. This all needed fixing before I could start with any analysis.

The dataset spans 24 Games from Chamonix 1924 to Beijing 2022, covering 1163 unique events. Not all NOCs appear in each Games, the number of competiting nations has grown over time, from around 19 nations in 1924 to over 90 today. 

```{python}
import pandas as pd
medals = pd.read_csv('../data/clean/medals_clean.csv')
print(f"{len(medals)} events scraped across {medals['year'].nunique()} Games")
medals.head(10)
```

---
## 2. Cleaning the Data
### 2.1 Sorting out the sport categories
The scraped HTML table included two types of rows: actual event results and section headings that just said the sport name. These headings were not real results, they had no medalists data or NOC codes, however they did have the sport name of each event which is information I still needed. 

To fix this, within the cleaning script (`clean.py`) I identified the heading rows which was any row where all medal columns were empty and then extracted the sport name. Allowing me to forward fill the sport name down to all the event rows below it until reaching the next heading. Then I could drop the original heading rows as they were no longer needed.
```{python}
print("Sports present in the dataset:")
print(sorted(medals['sport'].dropna().unique().tolist()))
```

### 2.2 Dealing with tied medals
Occasionally there were two countries that shared a podium position, for example two gold medalists and no silver. In the raw data these appear as a single combined entry in the NOC column rather than as two seperate rows. 

These needed to be detected and split into two rows, otherwise any country-level analysis would be inaccurate. Within the cleaning step these are flagged using the `tie` columns and then are split into seperate rows in a later step in the pipeline, so that both countries get the credit for the medal in the analysis.

```{python}
tied = medals[medals['tie_gold'] | medals['tie_silver'] | medals['tie_bronze']]
print(f"Events with at least one tied medal: {len(tied)}")
tied[['year', 'sport', 'event', 'tie_gold', 'tie_silver', 'tie_bronze']].head(8)
```


There are relatively few tied medals within the data however just ignoring them would mean undercounting the total medals for the countries involved and would lead to skewed results from the analysis.

---

## 3. Adding Economic Context
This is all great, but to answer the research question I also need to add some economic context to the medal data. To test if wealth or population drives success, each medal row needs to be merged with the GDP and population data for the corresponding country and year.

### 3.1 Why I used the World Bank API
I needed two indicators for each country and year: GDP per capita and population size. The World Bank API provided a convinent way to access this data for all the countries and years in my medal dataset. The advantage of using the World Bank for both is that the data is already cleaned and standardised, meaning I didn't have to worry about different country name formats or different structures for the data. 

They were both fetched directly from the World Bank API (https://data.worldbank.org/) using the `wbgapi` Python package, allowing for easy integration into the data pipeline.

### 3.2 The NOC to ISC problem
There was however a problem with merging the medal data with the World Bank data, as the medal data uses NOC codes to identify countries, whereas the World Bank data uses ISO country codes. This meant that I needed to find a way to map between the two code systems in order to merge the datasets together as well as the problem of some NOCs no longer existing or being split into multiple countries over time. So I had to create a lookup table to map each NOC to its cloests modern equivalent, so that historical medals can still be joined to economic data where possible. 

The trickest cases were the Soviet Union (URS), East Germany (GDR) and Yugoslavia (YUG) which are all heavy medal winners that no longer exists. For these I mapped to their largest successor state (Russia, Germany and Serbia) where economic data was available and excluded rows where I could not make a reasonable match. 

### 3.3 Merging the datasets
Once both datasets used the same country codes, they were joined on country and year. This allowed me to have a single dataset that included the medal information alongside the economic indicators for each country and year which I could then use for analysis. Alongside this I created two additional columns: 

- **`is_host`** : a flag marking whether the winning country was hosting the games that year, to test the impact of home advantage 
- **`log_gdp_per_capita`** and **`log_population`**: I took the log of both GDP per Capita and population to make the data more normally distributed, as the difference between a very poor and a moderately poor country is much more significant than the difference between a moderately rich and a very rich country, so taking the log allows for a more meaningful analysis of the relationship between wealth and medal success.

```{python}
final = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/worldbank_final.csv")
print(f"Final merged dataset: {len(final)} medal rows")
final.head(8)
```
```{python}
missing = final['gdp_per_capita'].isna().sum()
total = len(final)
print(f'Rows with missing GDP per capita: {missing} ({100 * missing / total:.1f}%)')
print('These are mostly historic NOCs')
```


The missing GDP rows are expected and unavoidable, as many of the older NOCs no longer exist and therefore do not have corresponding economic data in the World Bank dataset. Therefore, these rows are excluded from the regression but kept in the raw medal count so the all-time table remains accurate.

---

## 4. The Analysis
### 4.1 Aggregating to country-year level 
The merged dataset currently has one row per medal won by a country. However for the regression analysis I need to have one row per country per Olympic year. So that for each country-year combination I have the total number of medals won and I keep the GDP per capita and population data for that country and year. 

### 4.2 OLS regression
With the data in this shape, an OLS regression was run to directly answer the research question: after controlling for GDP per capita and population size, does hosting the games have a significant impact on medal success?
The model is: 
>**Total Medals = beta0 + beta1(is_host) + beta2(log GDP per capita) + beta3(log population) + epsilon**

```{python}
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
reg_data = (pd.read_csv('../data/clean/medals_country_year.csv'))
results_df = (pd.read_csv('../outputs/regression_results.csv'))
print(f"Rows in regression dataset: {len(reg_data)}")
print(f"Countries represented: {reg_data['noc'].nunique()}")
print(f"Host-country observations: {reg_data['is_host'].sum()}")
print()
print(results_df.to_string(index=False))

model = smf.ols('total_medals ~ is_host + log_gdp_per_capita + log_population', data=reg_data).fit()
print(model.summary())
```
The key result, is_host, has a coefficient of approximately 4.6, meaning a host nation wins around 4.6 more medals than it otherwise would, holding GDP and population constant. Both log_gdp_per_capita and log_population are highly significant (p < 0.001), confirming that wealth and size independently drive performance. 

## 5. Visualising the Results
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid', font_scale=1.1)
GOLD   = "#FFD700"
SILVER = "#C0C0C0"
BRONZE = "#CD7F32"
BLUE   = "#1f77b4"
alltime = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/alltime_table.csv")
cy      = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/medals_country_year.csv")
reg     = pd.read_csv("../outputs/regression_results.csv")
final   = pd.read_csv("../data/clean/worldbank_final.csv")
```
### 5.1 All-time Medal table
The chart shows cumulative medals since 1924. Norwary leads confortably, reflecting its deep winter sports culture and consistent investment in training. The US and Germany follow, benefitting from both size and sustained investment in winter sport.
```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

ax.barh(alltime['noc'], alltime['gold_medals'], label='Gold', color=GOLD, height=0.6)
ax.barh(alltime['noc'], alltime['silver_medals'], label='Silver', color=SILVER, height=0.6, left=alltime['gold_medals'])
ax.barh(alltime['noc'], alltime['bronze_medals'], label='Bronze', color=BRONZE, height=0.6, left=alltime['gold_medals'] + alltime['silver_medals'])
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_title("All time Winter Olympics Medal Table (top 20)", fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Total Medals Won")
ax.set_ylabel("Country")
ax.legend(loc='lower right')
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### 5.2 The Fallen Powers
The all-time table is dominated by nations that were strong in the early era of the Winter Olympics, but not all of them maintained that dominance.
The chart below compares each countries average medals per Games before 1980 against their average since 1994, for the top 10 nations of the early era.
The most striking decline is the Soviet Union's successor states and East Germany, which could be as a result of political and economic disruptions after the Cold War. Norway and the United States however have maintained or even grown their medal counts, reflecting sustained long-term investment in Winter sports. 
```{python}
cy_all = final.groupby(['year', 'noc'], as_index=False).agg(total_medals=('noc', 'count'))

early = cy_all[cy_all['year'] <= 1980]
modern = cy_all[cy_all['year'] >= 1994]
early_avg = early.groupby('noc')['total_medals'].mean().rename('early_avg').reset_index()
modern_avg = modern.groupby('noc')['total_medals'].mean().rename('modern_avg').reset_index()

top_early = early_avg.nlargest(10, 'early_avg')['noc']
compare = pd.merge(early_avg[early_avg['noc'].isin(top_early)], modern_avg, on='noc', how='left').fillna(0)
compare['decline'] = compare['early_avg'] - compare['modern_avg']
compare = compare.sort_values('decline', ascending=False)

long = compare.melt(id_vars=['noc'], value_vars=['early_avg', 'modern_avg'], var_name='era', value_name='avg_medals')
long['era'] = long['era'].map({'early_avg': 'Pre-1980 average', 'modern_avg': 'Post-1992 average'})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.barplot(data=long, x='noc', y='avg_medals', hue='era', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Early Dominance vs Modern Performance\n(Top 10 Pre-1980 Nations)', fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Country')
ax.set_ylabel('Average Medals per Games')
ax.legend(title='Era')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


### 5.3 How medal dominance has changed over time
The all-time medal table masks important changes over time. The chart shows the medal counts per Games for the top 6 nations. 
```{python}
top6   = cy.groupby('noc')['total_medals'].sum().nlargest(6).index.tolist()
subset = cy[cy['noc'].isin(top6)]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 6))
sns.lineplot(data=subset, x='year', y='total_medals',
             hue='noc', marker='o', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Winter Olympic Medal Count per Games (Top 6 Nations)',
             fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Year')
ax.set_ylabel('Medals Won')
ax.legend(title='Country', bbox_to_anchor=(1.01, 1),
          loc='upper left', borderaxespad=0.)
ax.set_xticks(sorted(cy['year'].unique()))
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### 5.4 The Wealth effects
The scatter plot shows the relationship between GDP per capita and medals won, across all country-year observations. The upward trend is clear, showing that wealthier countries tend to win more medals. This is consistent with the idea that richer countries have more resources to invest in training and facilities, which tend to lead to better performance in the Olympics. However, there are some outliers......
```{python}
plot_data = cy.dropna(subset=['log_gdp_per_capita', 'total_medals'])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.regplot(data=plot_data, x='log_gdp_per_capita', y='total_medals',
            scatter_kws={'alpha': 0.4, 's': 25, 'color': BLUE},
            line_kws={'color': 'red'}, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Wealthier Countries Win More Medals", fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Log GDP per Capita")
ax.set_ylabel("Total Medals Won")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### 5.5 Home Advantage
To visualise the home advantage effect, I created a bar chart comparing each host nations medal count in its home Games against its average performance in non-host years. Most host nations perform notably better at home, suggesting that familiarity with venues, crowd support and reduced travel fatigue may all contribute to improved performance. However, the size of the home advantage effect varies widely between countries, with some hosts seeing a much bigger boost than others.
```{python}
hosts = (cy[cy['is_host'] == 1][['noc', 'year', 'total_medals']].rename(columns={'total_medals': 'host_medals'}))
non_host_avg = (cy[cy['is_host'] == 0].groupby('noc')['total_medals'].mean().reset_index().rename(columns={'total_medals': 'avg_non_host'}))
compare = (pd.merge(hosts, non_host_avg, on='noc', how='inner').sort_values('host_medals', ascending=False).head(15))
long = compare.melt(id_vars=['noc'],value_vars=['host_medals', 'avg_non_host'], var_name='type', value_name='medals')
long['type'] = long['type'].map({'host_medals':  'Host Year','avg_non_host': 'Average Non-Host Year'})
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7))
sns.barplot(data=long, x='noc', y='medals', hue='type', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Medals when Hosting vs Not Hosting (Top 15 Host Nations)',fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel('Country')
ax.set_ylabel('Total Medals')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### 5.6 What the regression tells us
All three predictors show positive coefficients, each bar sits completely on the right of the zero line. The size of each bar shows the predicted extra medals per unit increase in that variable. GDP per capita has the largest effect, followed by host status and then population.

```{python}
key_vars = ['is_host', 'log_gdp_per_capita', 'log_population']
reg_plot = reg[reg['variable'].isin(key_vars)].copy()
reg_plot['variable'] = reg_plot['variable'].map({'is_host': 'Host Country', 'log_gdp_per_capita': 'Log GDP per Capita', 'log_population': 'Log Population'})
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.barh(reg_plot['variable'], reg_plot['Coef.'])
ax.errorbar(reg_plot['Coef.'], reg_plot['variable'], xerr=[
        reg_plot['Coef.'] - reg_plot['[0.025'],
        reg_plot['0.975]'] - reg_plot['Coef.']],fmt='none',color='black',capsize=4)

ax.axvline(0, color='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_title("What Predicts Winter Olympic Success?", fontsize=14, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Regression Coefficient (extra medals per unit increase)")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

---
## 6. Conclusions
Three factors were tested: wealth, population size and hosting the Games. All three are statistically significant predictors of medal count.

**Host advantage is real but limited**
There is definitely a home advantage, on average, host countries win about 4.6 more medals than we would expect based on their wealth and population alone (p=0.015). So this is a genuine effect, you can see it in cases such as USA in 2002 (34 medals) and Norway in 1994 (26 medals). That said, this effect is quite limited, a country only hosts once in a while, so even though the boost is real, it only applies to a single Olympic Games at a time.

**GDP per capita is the strongest overall predictor**
From the regression, a 1 unit increase in log GDP per capita is associated with about 2.7 model medals (p < 0.001). Which might not sound very large, however across the full range of countries in the dataset, it adds up to around a 16 medal difference between poorer and richer nations. This reflects the resources wealthy nations invest in training infrastructure, coaching and athlete development.

**Why the host bar looks largest on the chart but isn't the strongest predictor**
On the coefficient plot, the host effect looks larger than GDP, which is misleading. The host coefficient (4.6) represents a one-time jump from not hosting to hosting. GDP on the other hand, applies to a variable that spans 6 units in the data, meaning its total real world effect (around 16 medals) is more than three times larger. The t-statistic for GDP (8.01) is also more than three times that of host status (2.44), confirming it is the more consistent and reliable predictor across the dataset

**Population matters independently of wealth**
Population also plays an important role, even after accounting for wealth. A 1 unit increase in log population leads to about 1.4 additional medals (p < 0.001). Across the full range of countries, this again adds up to roughly a 16 medal difference. So even though the effect per unit is smaller than GDP, the overall impact is similar due to population varying so much between countries. 


---
